name: Binary
description: Base-2 numeral system using only digits 0 and 1. Each digit represents a power of 2 (bit). The fundamental number system of digital computers since all electronic logic can be represented as on/off states.
use_cases: CPU instruction sets and machine code
Data storage at the hardware level
Network subnet masks and IP addressing

name: Octal
description: Base-8 numeral system using digits 0 through 7. Each octal digit corresponds to exactly 3 binary bits, making it a compact representation of binary. Historically used in computing before hexadecimal became dominant.
use_cases: Compact representation of binary groups of 3 bits

name: Decimal
description: Base-10 numeral system using digits 0 through 9. The standard system for everyday arithmetic, likely originating from counting on ten fingers. All other positional number systems are typically converted to/from decimal for human comprehension.
use_cases: Default number system in most programming contexts

name: Hexadecimal
description: Base-16 numeral system using digits 0-9 and letters A-F (representing 10-15). Each hex digit represents exactly 4 binary bits (one nibble), making it extremely convenient for representing binary data compactly. Universally used in computing for memory addresses and color codes.
use_cases: Memory addresses and pointers in debugging
RGB color codes in web and graphics (e.g., #FF5733)
Byte representation in hex editors and network dumps
CPU register and hardware register values

name: Base32
description: Base-32 encoding using uppercase letters A-Z and digits 2-7, defined in RFC 4648. Encodes binary data into a subset of printable ASCII characters that are case-insensitive and avoid visually ambiguous characters.
use_cases: TOTP and HOTP secret keys in two-factor authentication (Google Authenticator)
Case-insensitive data encoding in file names
Encoded data in environments where lowercase is unavailable

name: Base64
description: Base-64 encoding using uppercase, lowercase letters, digits, plus (+) and slash (/), with = as padding, defined in RFC 4648. Encodes binary data into ASCII text, increasing size by ~33% but ensuring safe transmission through text-based protocols.
use_cases: Embedding binary data in JSON, XML, or HTML
Data URIs for embedding images in CSS/HTML
PEM-encoded TLS certificates and SSH keys
